skill.name: state-management
skill.description: Guides frontend developers in selecting and implementing state management patterns for React applications, including Context API, Zustand, Redux Toolkit, and TanStack Query.
skill.summary: Select and implement practical React state management patterns for local, global, and server state.
skill.author: supercent-io

security_audit.summary: Static analysis produced a high score mostly from Markdown code fences, npm install examples, relative API calls, and relative documentation links. No malicious intent, prompt injection, external exfiltration, or executable skill script was found. The confirmed concern is an auth example that stores a token in localStorage, which is risky guidance for browser applications.

medium_findings.title: Risky Browser Token Storage Example
medium_findings.description: The authentication Context example stores an auth token in localStorage. Persistent browser storage is accessible to injected JavaScript, so this is risky guidance for access tokens unless paired with strong XSS controls and a clear threat model.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The localStorage token write is explicit and appears in an authentication example. The issue is insecure instructional guidance, not evidence of malicious skill execution.

low_findings.title: Markdown Code Fences Flagged as Command Execution
low_findings.description: Most external command alerts come from Markdown fences and TypeScript examples. The only real shell commands are npm install instructions for Zustand and Redux Toolkit, which are ordinary setup steps and not executed by the skill.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The flagged backticks are Markdown formatting around examples. No file contains a runnable script or instruction to execute dynamic user-controlled shell input.

low_findings.title: Relative API Fetch Examples Without External Exfiltration
low_findings.description: The fetch calls use relative application endpoints for login, todos, and user profile examples. They demonstrate normal frontend API access and do not send data to hardcoded third-party domains.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The endpoints are relative paths under /api. Legitimate frontend state examples commonly include relative fetch calls, and no external destination is present.

low_findings.title: Relative Documentation Links Flagged as Path Traversal
low_findings.description: The path traversal alerts come from related-skill Markdown links that use ../ and ../../. They are documentation references, not filesystem reads or writes.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The suspicious sequences appear only in Markdown links under Related Skills. No code path uses them for file access.

dangerous_patterns.title: Browser Storage Used for Authentication Token
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes a browser localStorage write for an auth token in an example. This pattern should be revised or warned against because injected scripts can read localStorage.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The storage call is direct and tied to an authentication token. The risk is well established for browser applications that may face XSS.

content.value_statement: Frontend apps become hard to maintain when state spreads across components and APIs. This skill guides tool selection and implementation patterns for Context, Zustand, Redux Toolkit, and React Query.
actual_capabilities: Helps decide when state should remain local or become global.
Shows a React Context pattern for lightweight shared authentication state.
Provides a Zustand store pattern for shopping cart state and persistence.
Demonstrates Redux Toolkit slices, async thunks, and store setup.
Covers TanStack Query patterns for server data fetching, caching, and mutation.
Lists state management rules for immutability, derived values, and selective subscriptions.
limitations: The examples focus on React and TypeScript applications.
The code samples are instructional and require adaptation before production use.
The skill does not provide a complete authentication security design.
It does not compare runtime performance across state libraries.

use_cases.title: Choose the Right State Tool
use_cases.description: Compare local state, Context, Zustand, Redux Toolkit, and React Query for a new frontend feature.

use_cases.title: Refactor Shared UI State
use_cases.description: Move repeated props and duplicated state into a cleaner shared state pattern.

use_cases.title: Standardize Team Patterns
use_cases.description: Create consistent rules for global state, server state, derived values, and subscriptions.

prompt_templates.title: Pick a State Pattern
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me choose a state management pattern for a React feature. The state is used by these components: [list components]. The data changes when: [events].

prompt_templates.title: Design a Zustand Store
prompt_templates.prompt: Design a Zustand store for [domain]. Include state fields, actions, derived values, and component usage guidance.

prompt_templates.title: Plan Redux Toolkit State
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a Redux Toolkit structure for [feature]. Include slices, async flows, selectors, and how components should dispatch updates.

prompt_templates.title: Audit State Architecture
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this React state architecture for duplication, stale server data, unnecessary global state, and subscription performance issues: [describe architecture].

output_examples.input: A shopping cart is shared by product cards, the header, and the checkout page.
output: Recommendation: use Zustand for concise global client state.
Expected state: cart items, quantity updates, item removal, cart clearing, and a derived total.
Implementation note: subscribe components only to the fields they render.

output_examples.input: A page loads user records from an API and updates a profile form.
output: Recommendation: use TanStack Query for server data and mutation handling.
Expected flow: fetch by user ID, cache the result, send updates, then invalidate the matching query.
Implementation note: keep editable form state separate from cached server state.

output_examples.input: A team has duplicated theme and language state across many components.
output_examples.output: Recommendation: use React Context for lightweight shared settings, then keep feature-specific state local where possible.

best_practices: Keep state local until more than one area truly needs it.
Store derived values as calculations instead of duplicated state.
Subscribe components only to the state fields they need.
anti_patterns: Putting every UI value into global state by default.
Passing props through many layers when a shared provider would be clearer.
Duplicating the same server data in separate client stores.

faq.question: When should I use this skill?
faq.answer: Use it when React state is shared across components, hard to trace, or mixed with server data.

faq.question: Does it support Redux Toolkit?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes guidance for slices, async thunks, store setup, and component usage.

faq.question: Does it replace React Query documentation?
faq.answer: No. It gives implementation direction, but official documentation should guide production API details.

faq.question: Can it help remove props drilling?
faq.answer: Yes. It helps decide whether Context, Zustand, or another shared state approach is appropriate.

faq.question: Is the authentication example production ready?
faq.answer: No. Review token storage and session security before using any authentication example in production.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code compatible workflows.
